title: Artemis II Launches — First Crewed Mission Beyond Low Earth Orbit Since Apollo 17
detail: NASA's SLS Block 1 rocket carrying the Orion spacecraft (callsign: Integrity) lifted off from Launch Complex 39B at Kennedy Space Center at 6:35:12 p.m. EDT, approximately 11 minutes after the opening of the two-hour launch window. This is the first time humans have traveled beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in December 1972 — a gap of 53 years. The countdown had two brief technical holds: a Flight Termination System ground hardware communications issue (resolved with legacy shuttle-era equipment) and a Launch Abort System battery sensor showing elevated temperature (determined to be an instrumentation anomaly). Weather conditions improved to 90% favorable by launch day. The crew — Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover (first person of color beyond LEO), Mission Specialist Christina Koch (first woman on lunar trajectory), and Mission Specialist Jeremy Hansen (CSA, first non-American on a lunar trajectory) — donned their orange Orion Crew Survival System suits at the O&C Building, signed the 'white room' wall per Gemini-era tradition, and boarded Orion at approximately 2:30 p.m. EDT. All four solid rocket boosters separated on schedule; Orion's solar arrays (63-foot wingspan) fully deployed post-launch. The spacecraft is on a trans-lunar trajectory for a 10-day lunar flyby mission.

media.caption: Artemis II lifts off from LC-39B — first crewed mission beyond LEO since Apollo 17

title: Artemis II Crew Boards Orion — Suit-Up, White Room Signing, Hatch Closure
detail: The Artemis II crew completed their pre-launch suit-up at the Operations and Checkout Building at Kennedy Space Center following Launch Director Charlie Blackwell-Thompson's afternoon weather briefing. Each of the four crew members — Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen — signed the 'white room' wall at LC-39B per the tradition established during the Gemini program, continuing through Apollo and Shuttle. Crew ingress began at approximately 2:30 p.m. EDT (L-04:00 hours). The Launch Abort System hatch was closed at approximately L-01:30 hours. Ground teams performed final closeout work on the crew access arm before its retraction at T-02:55 hours.

sources.name: NASA Artemis Blog: Live Artemis II Launch Day Updates (Apr 1, 2026)
